title: Async Fluency
narrativeIntro: There's an easier way to speak with time. Instead of chaining and nesting, you can simply wait. The word is literal: await. Tell the system you're willing to pause, and it gives you the value when it's ready. The code reads like prose instead of machinery.
narrativeOutro: The code you write now reads like a story: do this, wait for that, then continue. No more nesting into oblivion. The structures in this region respond to you fluidly, as if you've learned their natural cadence.
phmConnectionNote: Ryland and Rocky develop a smoother communication system. You learn async/await -- the cleaner syntax for promises.
conceptsPreview: Async/await syntax and asynchronous error handling

challenges.title: Await Result
challenges.narrativeHook: An async function awaits a promise. What value comes out the other side?
challenges.instructions:
Read the code below. An async function awaits a resolved promise and returns the value. The result is then logged via `.then()`.

Without running it, trace what `await Promise.resolve("ready")` produces, what the function returns, and what `.then()` receives.
challenges.solution:
// No code changes — this is a prediction challenge.
// The answer is: "ready"
hints: await unwraps the promise and gives you the resolved value directly.
await Promise.resolve('ready') evaluates to the string 'ready'.
An async function wraps its return value in a promise, so .then() receives 'ready'.

challenges.title: Rewrite as Async
challenges.narrativeHook: A promise chain works but the nesting is growing. Rewrite it as readable async/await.
challenges.instructions:
The commented-out promise chain fetches a user then their posts. Rewrite `loadUserPosts` using async/await so it returns the number of posts.

The simulated API functions are read-only.

tests.description: loadUserPosts should return 2 (the number of posts)
tests.testCode: const result = await loadUserPosts(); if (result !== 2) throw new Error(`Expected 2, got ${result}`);

tests.description: loadUserPosts should be an async function
tests.testCode: if (loadUserPosts.constructor.name !== 'AsyncFunction') throw new Error('loadUserPosts should be declared with the async keyword');

hints: Add the async keyword before the function: async function loadUserPosts() { ... }
Use const user = await fetchUser(1); instead of .then(user => ...)
Wrap in try/catch to handle errors instead of .catch()

challenges.title: Missing Async
challenges.narrativeHook: The await is there but the function isn't async. The system chokes.
challenges.instructions:
This function uses `await` to get crew data and return the name. But it crashes with a syntax error.

There is exactly one bug. Find it and fix it.

tests.description: getCrewData should be an async function
tests.testCode: if (getCrewData.constructor.name !== 'AsyncFunction') throw new Error('getCrewData must be declared with the async keyword');
await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, 10)); if (crewName !== 'Grace') throw new Error(`crewName should be 'Grace', got '${crewName}'`);

explainGate.prompt: What does the async keyword do, and why is it required when using await?

hints: You can only use await inside a function declared with async.
Add the async keyword before the function keyword on line 3.
async function getCrewData() { ... } — that's the only change needed.

challenges.title: Safe Load
challenges.narrativeHook: Fetch data, handle the loading state, handle the error state.
challenges.instructions:
Write an async function `safeLoad` that takes any promise and returns a result object:
- If the promise resolves: return `{ ok: true, data: value }`
- If the promise rejects: return `{ ok: false, error: message }`

Use try/catch to handle both cases.

tests.description: Should return { ok: true, data: 'connected' } for a resolving promise
tests.testCode: const r = await safeLoad(Promise.resolve('connected')); if (!r.ok || r.data !== 'connected') throw new Error(`Expected { ok: true, data: 'connected' }, got ${JSON.stringify(r)}`);

tests.description: Should return { ok: false, error: 'timeout' } for a rejecting promise
tests.testCode: const r = await safeLoad(Promise.reject('timeout')); if (r.ok || r.error !== 'timeout') throw new Error(`Expected { ok: false, error: 'timeout' }, got ${JSON.stringify(r)}`);

explainGate.prompt: What is the role of try/catch inside an async function?

hints: Use try { const value = await promise; } to handle the resolved case.
Use catch (err) { } to intercept the rejected case -- err receives the rejection value.
Return the appropriate object from each branch: { ok: true, data: value } or { ok: false, error: err }.
